section: Evaluation of change in material properties due to plastic deformation

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the study objectives and presents macroscopic tensile tests on SPCE steel to measure the elastic modulus change after plastic deformation. Using a precision extensometer, the Young's modulus was found to decrease with plastic strain, dropping more than 20% at 25% strain (Fig. 1). V-bending simulations incorporating the plastic-strain-dependent modulus predicted spring-back more accurately than a constant modulus (Fig. 2). Possible causes—residual stress, micro-cracks, and dislocation pile-up—are discussed, with emphasis on movable dislocations and the Cottrell effect. To investigate the role of movable dislocations, SPCC specimens (Table 1) were subjected to heat treatment at 373 K for 90 h to recover the Cottrell atmosphere while keeping dislocations stationary. Stress-strain curves and modulus gradients were measured (Fig. 4, Table 2), revealing that the annealed specimen maintained a nearly constant modulus up to yield, whereas pre-strained samples showed a decreasing modulus from the start. The pre-strained plus heat-treated sample exhibited a gradient recovery toward the annealed level, confirming the significant role of movable dislocations in modulus reduction. The next section will address nano-indentation to measure local modulus distribution within grains.
material_systems: SPCC steel (used in heat treatment dislocation study)
processing_route: uniaxial tensile pre-straining up to 25% plastic strain
precision tensile test with extensometer and strain gauges
heat treatment at 373 K for 90 h to recover Cottrell atmosphere
nano-indentation (to be described in later chunks)
key_variables: heat treatment temperature (373 K) and time (90 h)
properties: SPCE Young's modulus: drops from 100% to 72% of initial value after 0.26 true strain
SPCC annealed: 224 GPa in 0-0.05% strain range; as-received: 182 GPa
pre-strained SPCC: reduced and strain-dependent modulus; recovery after heat treatment
mechanisms: movable dislocations reduce apparent elastic modulus during unloading
Cottrell effect locks dislocations before deformation; its recovery restrains movable dislocations and restores modulus
residual stress and minor micro-cracks also contribute but play lesser roles

Key variables: plastic true strain (up to 0.26); Young's modulus variation δE/E0 (%); spring-back angle after V-bending; heat treatment temperature (373 K) and time (90 h); dislocation pile-up at grain boundaries; Cottrell effect (carbon locking of dislocations)
Microstructure: dislocation pile-up at grain boundaries; Cottrell atmosphere (interstitial carbon locking dislocations); micro-cracks (minor effect, <1% density reduction)

section: 2. Measurement of the elastic modulus after large deformation
previous_context_summary:

context_update.summary: This chunk describes the nano-indentation experimental setup for measuring local elastic modulus. A Triboscope nano-indenter on an AFM stage was used, with force range 1–10 mN and displacement range 5 μm (Table 3). To avoid plastic deformation artifacts, a spherical indenter tip with nominal radius 50 μm was employed, resulting in purely elastic indentation; a representative load–displacement curve showed penetration depth of ~10 nm at 350 μN (Fig. 8). Nano-indentation near grain boundaries on SPCE steel revealed that the elastic modulus is lower near the boundary than in the interior, varying with grain orientation (Fig. 9). Surface preparation involved etching, Vickers marking, and CMP treatment to minimize roughness (Figs. 10,11). Post-CMP measurements over a 20×3 grid (1 μm spacing) confirmed a Young's modulus decrease in the X=5–10 μm boundary zone; average values were lower than earlier tests due to the real tip radius being smaller than nominal, but this offset can be calibrated using standards (Fig. 12). Subsequent analysis may discuss calibration, dislocation mechanisms, and quantitative modulus reduction.
processing_route: Vickers hardness marking near targeted grain boundary
Nano-indentation with spherical tip (R=50 μm) using Triboscope on AFM
Measurement grid: X range 0-20 μm, 1 μm interval, Y=0,1,2
key_variables: Local Young's modulus near grain boundary: reduced in X=5-10 μm
Average modulus lower due to real tip radius < nominal; calibration needed
microstructure: Movable dislocations more probable near boundaries
properties: Local elastic modulus near grain boundary is smaller than interior
Young's modulus decrease at boundary zone X=5-10 μm
Average modulus values biased low due to tip radius error
mechanisms: Pile-up of dislocations leads to spatial variation of elastic modulus within a grain
Spherical indenter tip avoids plastic deformation so measured modulus reflects pre-existing material state
Real tip radius < nominal reduces apparent modulus

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: The next section will address nano-indentation to measure local modulus distribution within grains. This chunk describes the nano-indentation experimental setup for measuring local elastic modulus. A Triboscope nano-indenter on an AFM stage was used, with force range 1–10 mN and displacement range 5 μm (Table 3). To avoid plastic deformation artifacts, a spherical indenter tip with nominal radius 50 μm was employed, resulting in purely elastic indentation; a representative load–displacement curve showed penetration depth of ~10 nm at 350 μN (Fig. 8). Nano-indentation near grain boundaries on SPCE steel revealed that the elastic modulus is lower near the boundary than in the interior, varying with grain orientation (Fig. 9). Surface preparation involved etching, Vickers marking, and CMP treatment to minimize roughness (Figs. 10,11). Post-CMP measurements over a 20×3 grid (1 μm spacing) confirmed a Young's modulus decrease in the X=5–10 μm boundary zone; average values were lower than earlier tests due to the real tip radius being smaller than nominal, but this offset can be calibrated using standards (Fig. 12). Subsequent analysis may discuss calibration, dislocation mechanisms, and quantitative modulus reduction.
Material systems: SPCE steel (ferritic low-carbon steel sheet); SPCC steel (used in heat treatment dislocation study); SPCE steel
Sample aliases: SPCE; SPCC
Processing route: uniaxial tensile pre-straining up to 25% plastic strain; precision tensile test with extensometer and strain gauges; V-bending test for spring-back measurement; heat treatment at 373 K for 90 h to recover Cottrell atmosphere; nano-indentation (to be described in later chunks); Etching for grain boundary disclosure; Vickers hardness marking near targeted grain boundary; CMP surface treatment
Key variables: plastic true strain (up to 0.26); Young's modulus variation δE/E0 (%); spring-back angle after V-bending; heat treatment temperature (373 K) and time (90 h); dislocation pile-up at grain boundaries; Cottrell effect (carbon locking of dislocations); Force range 1-10 mN, sensitivity 100 nN; Displacement range 5 μm, sensitivity 0.2 nm
Microstructure: dislocation pile-up at grain boundaries; Cottrell atmosphere (interstitial carbon locking dislocations); micro-cracks (minor effect, <1% density reduction); Dislocation pile-up near grain boundaries; Movable dislocations more probable near boundaries; Grain orientation affects modulus level
Properties: SPCE Young's modulus: drops from 100% to 72% of initial value after 0.26 true strain; SPCC annealed: 224 GPa in 0-0.05% strain range; as-received: 182 GPa; pre-strained SPCC: reduced and strain-dependent modulus; recovery after heat treatment; Local elastic modulus near grain boundary is smaller than interior; Young's modulus decrease at boundary zone X=5-10 μm; Average modulus values biased low due to tip radius error

context_update.summary: This chunk concludes the nano-indentation section with the quantification of local Young's modulus decrease near grain boundaries. It reports that the decrease occurs within X=5–10 μm of the boundary, and notes that the average measured moduli are lower than previous tests due to the actual tip radius being smaller than nominal; this offset can be calibrated with standard samples. The chunk then presents the paper's conclusions: elastic modulus was measured before/after plastic deformation to evaluate spring-back influence; microscopic modulus within a grain was measured by nano-indentation; apparent elastic modulus decreases with increasing plastic strain; movable dislocations accompanying pile-up near grain boundaries are a major factor; modulus decrease near grain boundaries was confirmed by nano-indentation; and the macroscopic behavior was verified by microscopic measurement. No figures or tables are referenced in this segment. The chunk effectively wraps up the study's key findings, linking processing, microstructure, and property changes.
processing_route: uniaxial tensile pre-straining up to 25% plastic strain
nano-indentation with spherical tip (nominal radius 50 μm)
calibration with standard samples to correct tip radius error
key_variables: real tip radius smaller than nominal
properties: elastic modulus decrease near grain boundary (X=5–10 μm zone)
apparent elastic modulus decreases with plastic strain
average nano-indentation modulus lower due to tip radius error
mechanisms: dislocation pile-up at grain boundary reduces local modulus
movable dislocations are a major factor for modulus decrease
